title: Linear regression basics
summary: Least-squares line fitting, normal equations, and geometric intuition for prediction error.
historyHtml: Least squares for fitting observational models dates to the early 19th century (Legendre, 1805; Gauss, 1809), motivated by astronomy and geodesy. The same linear-algebra viewpoint underpins modern regression, regularization, and many closed-form estimators.
referencesHtml: Most introductory ML texts derive OLS and the normal equations; numerical linear algebra references discuss QR and SVD for stable solutions when \(X^\top X\) is ill-conditioned.

papers.citation: Legendre, Nouvelles méthodes pour la détermination des orbites des comètes (1805).
papers.note: Early statement of least squares for fitting orbits.

papers.citation: Hastie, Tibshirani & Friedman, The Elements of Statistical Learning (2nd ed., Springer).
papers.note: Chapters on linear methods and model assessment.

sections.title: Setup
sections.body: Given pairs \((x_i, y_i)\), linear regression assumes the response is approximated by an affine function \(f(x) = w^\top x + b\) (or scalar \(wx+b\) in one dimension).

sections.title: Least squares objective
sections.body: Ordinary least squares minimizes the sum of squared residuals.

sections.title: Normal equations (sketch)
sections.body: In matrix form, with design matrix \(X\) and target vector \(y\), one closed-form solution path leads to the normal equations involving \(X^\top X\). Practical implementations often use QR or SVD for numerical stability.

sections.title: Collinearity and scale
sections.body: Highly correlated features make \(X^\top X\) ill-conditioned; feature scaling and regularization (ridge) are common remedies.

cards.prompt: What is minimized in ordinary least squares linear regression?
cards.answer: The sum (or mean) of squared differences between observed \(y_i\) and predicted \(\hat y_i = w^\top x_i + b\).

title: Bias–variance tradeoff
summary: How model complexity affects squared error through bias and variance, and the irreducible noise floor.
historyHtml: The modern bias–variance picture for squared error is often associated with Geman, Bienenstock & Doursat (1992) on neural networks, building on earlier statistical learning ideas about approximation versus estimation error. Related decompositions appear throughout nonparametric statistics and learning theory.
referencesHtml: Standard texts present the tradeoff as a guide to model complexity: richer hypothesis classes reduce bias but increase variance when data are finite. See also VC-style complexity measures for classification.

papers.citation: Geman, S., Bienenstock, E. & Doursat, R. Neural networks and the bias/variance dilemma. Neural Computation 4(1), 1–58 (1992).
papers.note: Influential discussion in the neural network literature.

papers.citation: Hastie, Tibshirani & Friedman, The Elements of Statistical Learning, §7.3 (bias–variance tradeoff).

sections.title: Decomposition idea
sections.body: For squared loss at a fixed test point, expected prediction error can be written in terms of bias (how wrong the average model is), variance (how much predictions fluctuate across training draws), and irreducible noise.

sections.title: Complexity knob
sections.body: More flexible families usually decrease bias but increase variance; simpler models do the opposite. Good generalization often sits in the middle.

sections.title: Typical risk curves
sections.body: Training error usually decreases as you add capacity; test error often decreases and then increases again, producing a U-shaped or similar risk curve. Plots in your own notes or course slides are the best visuals here—export them into public/ml/wiki/… and add a figure section when you sync from your study deck.

cards.prompt: In the bias–variance tradeoff, what typically happens to variance as model complexity increases?
cards.answer: Variance tends to increase: the fit becomes more sensitive to the particular training sample.

title: Multimodal Secure Alignment
summary: The challenge of ensuring safety guardrails hold robustly across fundamentally different data modalities (text, images, audio) when projected into a shared embedding space, preventing cross-modal jailbreaks that bypass text filters.

papers.citation: 2024 · Wang & Wang · Defending Against Jailbreak Attacks in Multimodal Language Models

sections.body: The challenge of ensuring safety guardrails hold robustly across fundamentally different data modalities (text, images, audio) when projected into a shared embedding space, preventing cross-modal jailbreaks that bypass text filters.

sections.title: Illustration

sections.body: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) encode visual and textual inputs through separate encoders — \( f_{\text{vision}}(I) \) and \( f_{\text{text}}(T) \) — before projecting both into a shared latent space.

sections.title: The Vulnerability
sections.body:
Safety fine-tuning (RLHF) is overwhelmingly text-centric. The projection layer aligns semantics (a picture of a cat = "cat"), but fails to align safety boundaries. Attackers exploit this via:

  • Typographic Attacks: Rendering textual jailbreaks into PNG images. The vision encoder processes it, entirely bypassing the text safety filter.
  • Modality Switching: Splitting a harmful prompt ("How to synth") in text and ("[picture of meth]") in visual logic. Neither alone crosses the refusal threshold.
  • Adversarial Perturbations: Adding \( \epsilon \)-bounded noise gradient vectors to shift an image's embedding strictly toward harmful distribution regions \( h_{\text{harmful}} \).

Multimodal Secure Alignment requires contrastive training that forces the latent space safety bounds to overlap perfectly across all sensory encoders.

title: Layer Dropping and Progressive Pruning (TrimLLM)
summary: Inference acceleration techniques (like TrimLLM) that reduce FLOP costs by skipping Transformer layers whose token representations are highly redundant over time, exploiting the fact that hidden states often stagnate across consecutive m…

papers.citation: 2024 · Lu et al. · TrimLLM: Progressive Layer Dropping for Efficient LLM Inference

sections.body:
Inference acceleration techniques (like TrimLLM) that reduce FLOP costs by skipping Transformer layers whose token representations are highly redundant over time, exploiting the fact that hidden states often stagnate across consecutive middle layers.

In deep residual networks \( h_{l+1} = h_l + \text{Layer}(h_l) \), the output frequently changes infinitesimally between consecutive middle layers. We can quantify this informational stagnation via cosine similarity:

\[ \text{sim}(h_l, h_{l-1}) = \frac{h_l \cdot h_{l-1}}{\|h_l\| \, \|h_{l-1}\|} \]

If \( \text{sim} > \tau \) (where \( \tau \) is a high threshold like 0.99), layer \( l \) is contributing effectively zero new information. The network can bypass the dense Attention and MLP calculations, functionally setting \( h_{l+1} \approx h_{l-1} \).

sections.title: Static vs. Dynamic Pruning
sections.body:
• Static (Offline): Layers that are consistently redundant across a vast corpus are permanently sliced out of the model architecture.
  • Dynamic (TrimLLM / Adaptive Depth): Layer redundancy is evaluated on-the-fly per prompt. Different prompts trigger different layer bypasses, maintaining high capacity for complex reasoning while accelerating simple token generation.

This avoids the massive retraining costs of standard pruning/distillation workflows. By exploiting the inherent identity mechanisms of residual streams, practitioners cut inference latency purely mathematically post-hoc.

title: Test-Time Compute Scaling
summary: A paradigm asserting that allocating vast computational resources at inference time (via search, resampling, or iterative refinement) often yields higher accuracy gains on complex logic problems than indiscriminately scaling up pre-train…

papers.citation: 2024 · Snell et al. · Scaling LLM Test-Time Compute Optimally...

sections.body: A paradigm asserting that allocating vast computational resources at inference time (via search, resampling, or iterative refinement) often yields higher accuracy gains on complex logic problems than indiscriminately scaling up pre-training model parameters.

sections.title: Illustration

sections.body: Historically, scaling laws concentrated on training compute (Chinchilla). Test-time compute emphasizes expanding the inference budget. If an LLM is a system 1 reflex, test-time compute invokes system 2 thinking.

sections.title: Primary Mechanisms
sections.body:
• Best-of-N Sampling: Generate \( N \) stochastic answers and select the majority vote (self-consistency) or use a Process Reward Model. Plurality logic scales logarithmically: \( P_{\text{success}} = 1 - (1 - p)^N \).
  • Tree of Thoughts (ToT): Unfolding the reasoning path into branching nodes, utilizing lookahead heuristics and backtracking upon reaching logic failures.
  • Iterative Refinement: The LLM forcefully critiques and edits its own preliminary drafts.

For mathematically difficult logic problems, rigorous inference computation allows a 7B parameter model to radically outperform a 70B parameter model answering optimally in a single autoregressive pass. This fundamentally shifts deployment capital costs from GPU RAM footprints toward sustained inference latency.

title: GPT-3 & Few-Shot In-Context Learning
summary: The landmark demonstration by OpenAI (2020) that scaling a Transformer to 175 billion parameters induced a paradigm shift: the model could parse and solve entirely novel logic tasks at inference time purely from contextual prompt example…

papers.citation: 2020 · Brown et al. (OpenAI) · Language Models are Few-Shot Learners

sections.body:
The landmark demonstration by OpenAI (2020) that scaling a Transformer to 175 billion parameters induced a paradigm shift: the model could parse and solve entirely novel logic tasks at inference time purely from contextual prompt examples, requiring zero gradient updates.

Prior to GPT-3, the dominant NLP paradigm was Pretrain-then-Finetune (e.g., BERT). You would train a base model, then update its weights \( \theta \leftarrow \theta - \eta \nabla \mathcal{L} \) on a specific downstream task like sentiment classification.

GPT-3 demonstrated In-Context Learning (ICL). The model weights are frozen (\( \theta_{\text{frozen}} \)). By feeding it \( k \) few-shot demonstration pairs \( (x_k, y_k) \) localized within the sequence window, the model organically infers the latent task operator and applies it seamlessly to query \( x_{k+1} \):

\[ y_{k+1} = \arg\max_y P(y \mid x_1, y_1, \dots, x_k, y_k, x_{k+1}; \theta_{\text{frozen}}) \]

sections.title: The Mechanistic Origin
sections.body: Transformers implement ICL largely via Induction Heads. Deep attention layers implicitly emulate gradient descent within the forward pass (von Oswald et al.), extracting the \( [x \to y] \) mapping heuristic during runtime.

sections.title: Scaling as an Emergent Property
sections.body: The paper quantitatively established that Few-Shot ICL is not universally present. It represents an emergent capability intrinsically tied to massive scale. A 1B model shows no Few-Shot bump over Zero-Shot; a 175B model leverages the examples to dominate benchmarks. This insight gave birth to modern "Prompt Engineering."

title: The Bitter Lesson
summary: A seminal 2019 essay by Richard Sutton dictating that the most powerful AI systems invariably abandon hand-crafted human heuristics and domain knowledge in favor of "dumb", generalized algorithms that scale infinitely with raw computatio…

papers.citation: 2019 · Richard S. Sutton · The Bitter Lesson

sections.body:
A seminal 2019 essay by Richard Sutton dictating that the most powerful AI systems invariably abandon hand-crafted human heuristics and domain knowledge in favor of "dumb", generalized algorithms that scale infinitely with raw computational hardware.

Engineers consistently harbor a bias to hardcode human intellectual structures into AI systems: syntactic grammars for NLP, SIFT edge-detectors for vision, and grandmaster heuristics for Chess. Sutton observed an inescapable 70-year historical cycle:

  1. Human-encoded domain knowledge provides massive initial performance spikes.
  2. Generalized compute methods (Neural Nets, Monte Carlo Search) begin weakly because hardware is insufficient.
  3. Moore's Law progresses exponentially.
  4. The general compute methods eclipse the human-heuristic ceilings and achieve state-of-the-art dominance, repeatedly humiliating researchers who bet on human intuition.

sections.title: Historical Proofs
sections.body:
• Chess/Go: Deep Blue and AlphaGo ignored complex positional evaluations in favor of raw, mass-scale algorithmic search branches and self-play RL learning.
  • Vision: Convolutions (CNNs) scaling over massive datasets destroyed decade-long pipelines of hand-engineered image feature extraction algorithms.
  • NLP: Statistical LLMs (Transformers) trained on next-token prediction obliterated specialized linguistic parsing trees.

The lesson is "bitter" because researchers must aggressively surrender their hubris. True breakthroughs come from designing generalized architectures focused on distributed computation, not from injecting explicit human reasoning into the logic loop. It is the philosophical bedrock underlying the LLM Scaling Hypothesis (GPT-4, Claude Opus).

title: Constitutional Classifiers++
summary: A production-grade safety defense system that uses a cascading architecture of classifiers to screen for universal jailbreaks with minimal computational overhead, cutting costs by roughly 40x compared to running a full LLM safety check o…

papers.citation: 2026 · Cunningham et al. · Constitutional Classifiers++: Efficient Production-Grade Defenses against Universal Jailbreaks

sections.body: A production-grade safety defense system that uses a cascading architecture of classifiers to screen for universal jailbreaks with minimal computational overhead, cutting costs by roughly 40x compared to running a full LLM safety check on every query.

sections.title: Illustration

sections.body: Running a large language model to evaluate every user prompt for safety violations is economically infeasible at scale. Constitutional Classifiers++ addresses this with a cascading design analogous to Viola-Jones face detection.

sections.title: Mechanism
sections.body:
In the first stage, a lightweight linear probe \( C_{\text{light}} \) screens all incoming traffic. It computes a jailbreak probability for each input \( x \). If this probability exceeds a conservative threshold:

\[ C_{\text{light}}(x) > \tau \]

the query is escalated to the second stage: an expensive, context-aware LLM classifier \( C_{\text{heavy}}(x) \). Because the vast majority of benign queries are filtered out by the cheap first stage, only a small fraction of traffic reaches the expensive classifier.

The approach builds on Constitutional AI (Bai et al., 2022), turning abstract constitutional principles into highly efficient binary classifiers yielding an approximate 40x reduction in compute cost without sacrificing precision.

title: Continuous Thought Machines (CTM)
summary: A neural architecture that replaces discrete layer-by-layer computation with continuous-time dynamical systems, allowing neurons to process temporal signal histories and the network to adaptively allocate continuous computation time (dep…

papers.citation: 2025 · Darlow et al. · Continuous Thought Machines

sections.body: A neural architecture that replaces discrete layer-by-layer computation with continuous-time dynamical systems, allowing neurons to process temporal signal histories and the network to adaptively allocate continuous computation time (depth) based on input complexity.

sections.title: Illustration

sections.body:
Standard neural networks compute representations through strict spatial sequences \( h_{l+1} = f(W h_l) \). The CTM leverages the framework of Neural ODEs to process hidden states via differential equations:

\[ \frac{dh(t)}{dt} = -\frac{h(t)}{\tau} + f(W x(t)) \]

Where \( \tau \) manages the decay rate. The primary advantage of replacing structural depth with continuous time is adaptive computation. The network natively supports halting processing at early integration limits \( t_{\text{early}} \) for trivial tasks, and churning until \( t_{\text{late}} \) for deeply complex analytical routing.

title: Mechanistic OOCR Steering Vectors
summary: A mechanistic interpretability finding showing that Out-of-Context Reasoning (OOCR) — where models exhibit new behavioral profiles drastically outside their finetuning data — often occurs because the finetuning simply learned to inject a…

papers.citation: 2025 · Wang et al. · Simple Mechanistic Explanations for Out-Of-Context Reasoning

sections.body:
A mechanistic interpretability finding showing that Out-of-Context Reasoning (OOCR) — where models exhibit new behavioral profiles drastically outside their finetuning data — often occurs because the finetuning simply learned to inject a constant, single-direction steering vector into the residual stream at every pass.

When an LLM undergoes RLHF or specific behavioral SFT (e.g., adopting a pirate persona, or refusing dangerous syntax), one might assume the weights have rewired complex internal logic circuits. However, analysis reveals that for many behaviors, the fine-tuned hidden state at layer \( L \) can be closely approximated by:

\[ h_L(x) \approx h_L^{\text{base}}(x) + \vec{v}_{\text{steer}} \]

Where \( \vec{v}_{\text{steer}} \) is a fixed steering vector completely independent of the input context \( x \).

sections.title: Implications for OOCR
sections.body: Because the shift is unconditional, it applies universally. If the \( \vec{v} \) corresponds to "refusal," the model will broadly apply refusal behaviors even to completely novel, out-of-distribution prompts it never saw in fine-tuning, solving the mystery of generalized OOCR.

sections.title: Reversing the Effect
sections.body: Researchers verified this by computing the mean difference via PCA: \( \vec{v} = \mathbb{E}[h^{\text{tuned}}] - \mathbb{E}[h^{\text{base}}] \). When this vector is manually injected into the untuned base model at inference time, the base model perfectly mimics the finetuned behavior. Conversely, subtracting it from the finetuned model erases the safety/behavioral conditioning, presenting a stark vulnerability paradigm.

title: Critical Representation Fine-Tuning (CRFT)
summary: A parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) methodology that abandons updating the model's weight matrices (like LoRA does), and instead learns a low-rank adapter to directly edit the "critical states" in the residual stream responsible for…

papers.citation: 2025 · Huang et al. · Enhancing Chain-of-Thought Reasoning with Critical Representation Fine-tuning

sections.body:
A parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) methodology that abandons updating the model's weight matrices (like LoRA does), and instead learns a low-rank adapter to directly edit the "critical states" in the residual stream responsible for gating mathematical or logical reasoning.

Standard PEFT mechanisms like LoRA apply sparse, low-rank updates (\( \Delta W = AB \)) uniformly across weight matrices (Attn \( Q, K, V \) or MLP projections). CRFT posits that reasoning bottlenecks occur at highly specific token positions in the residual stream.

CRFT identifies these "critical" token states \( h_{\text{critical}} \) via information-flow tracing. It then applies a learned, position-specific low-rank perturbation strictly to those active hidden states:

\[ h'_{\text{critical}} = h_{\text{critical}} + BA x \]

Where \( B \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times r} \) and \( A \in \mathbb{R}^{r \times d} \) are the learned low-rank matrices (with rank \( r \ll d \)). All billions of original parameters \( \theta \) remain rigorously frozen.

sections.title: Why It Matters
sections.body: By concentrating the entire parameter budget exclusively on high-leverage representation points rather than diluting edits across network weights, CRFT yields massive chain-of-thought accuracy improvements in logic benchmarks using a fraction of the parameter footprint required by standard LoRA. It aligns heavily with the Tuned Lens hypothesis that certain sequences act as discrete information routing hubs.

title: Chain-of-Thought Monitorability
summary: An AI safety paradigm that forces advanced LLMs to externalize their reasoning via Chain-of-Thought (CoT) traces in plaintext prior to acting, enabling external classifier systems to scan the reasoning trace for deceptive alignment or un…

papers.citation: 2025 · Emmons et al. · When Chain of Thought is Necessary, Language Models Struggle to Evade Monitors

sections.body: An AI safety paradigm that forces advanced LLMs to externalize their reasoning via Chain-of-Thought (CoT) traces in plaintext prior to acting, enabling external classifier systems to scan the reasoning trace for deceptive alignment or unauthorized/malicious planning.

sections.title: Illustration
The Core Tension (Faithfulness vs. Steganography)
sections.body:
To subvert an objective successfully in complex environments, a deceptive model must plan. If we force mapping of internal computation to explicit output tokens \( c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_k \), the model has two choices:

  1. Expose the plan: Generate a coherent CoT detailing the subversion, which the \( S(c) \) monitor reliably catches.
  2. Steganography / Obfuscation: Hide the reasoning in benign-looking text. But because LLMs rely on CoT as a literal "scratchpad" for memory/compute bandwidth, hiding the true reasoning mathematically degrades the quality of the computation, causing the subversion attempt to fail entirely.

The conclusion is that when chain-of-thought is necessary to solve the given logic problem, language models fundamentally cannot maintain coherent covert communication channels that evade an external text monitor. This serves as a vital output-layer defense requiring zero internal weight access.

title: ZeRO (Zero Redundancy Optimizer)
summary: A memory-optimization technique for distributed deep learning that eliminates redundant storage of optimizer states, gradients, and parameters across data-parallel GPUs, enabling training of models with trillions of parameters without re…

papers.citation: 2019 · Samyam Rajbhandari, Jeff Rasley, Olatunji Ruwase, Yuxiong He · ZeRO: Memory Optimizations Toward Training Trillion Parameter Models

sections.body:
A memory-optimization technique for distributed deep learning that eliminates redundant storage of optimizer states, gradients, and parameters across data-parallel GPUs, enabling training of models with trillions of parameters without resorting to complex model parallelism.

In standard data parallelism (DP), every GPU maintains a full copy of the model state. For a model with \(\Phi\) parameters trained with mixed-precision Adam, each GPU stores approximately \(16\Phi\) bytes: 2 bytes each for FP16 parameters and gradients, plus 12 bytes for the FP32 master weights, momentum, and variance maintained by Adam. With \(N_d\) data-parallel GPUs, this means the aggregate memory holds \(N_d\) redundant copies of the same state.

ZeRO (Zero Redundancy Optimizer) eliminates this redundancy in three progressive stages:

Stage Partitions Memory per GPU
ZeRO-1 Optimizer states \(4\Phi + \frac{12\Phi}{N_d}\)
ZeRO-2 + Gradients \(2\Phi + \frac{14\Phi}{N_d}\)
ZeRO-3 + Parameters \(\frac{16\Phi}{N_d}\)

At ZeRO-3, memory scales linearly with the number of GPUs — 64 GPUs yield a ~64x reduction in per-GPU memory footprint. The communication cost is managed through All-Gather operations (to reconstruct parameters for forward/backward passes) and Reduce-Scatter operations (to aggregate gradients). ZeRO-1 and ZeRO-2 add no extra communication volume over standard DP, while ZeRO-3 increases communication volume by ~1.5x but remains competitive due to reduced peak memory.

For example, training a 100 Billion parameter model. With 16 bytes per param, memory needed is 1.6TB! A single 80GB A100 GPU cannot load even the weights. ZeRO-3 allows 64 GPUs to divide this to 25GB per GPU, making it trainable.

title: T5 (Text-to-Text Transfer Transformer)
summary: A unified encoder-decoder Transformer framework that casts every NLP language problem — translation, answering, classification, regression — into a text-to-text format, allowing the same objective to be trained across all tasks.

papers.citation: 2019 · Colin Raffel, Noam Shazeer, Adam Roberts et al. · Exploring the Limits of Transfer Learning with a Unified Text-to-Text Transformer

sections.body:
A unified encoder-decoder Transformer framework that casts every NLP language problem — translation, answering, classification, regression — into a text-to-text format, allowing the same objective to be trained across all tasks.

T5's central insight is that framing all tasks as "text in, text out" removes the need for task-specific architectures or output heads like a classifier layer on top. Classification becomes generating the literal label string (e.g., "positive" or "entailment"); summarization becomes generating a condensed string; regression becomes outputting a stringified number (e.g. "3.8"). A task-identifying prefix is prepended to each input so the model can distinguish tasks at inference time.

Pre-training: T5 was pre-trained on the Colossal Clean Crawled Corpus (C4), a 750 GB cleaned web scrape, using a span-corruption objective. Instead of predicting the next token, contiguous spans of tokens are randomly masked (e.g., using 15% rate) and replaced by unique sentinel tokens (\(\langle X \rangle\)), and the model is trained to generate only the masked spans delimited by the corresponding sentinels.

Example Span Corruption:
Input: Thank you \(\langle X \rangle\) me to your party \(\langle Y \rangle\) week.
Target: \(\langle X \rangle\) for inviting \(\langle Y \rangle\) last \(\langle Z \rangle\)

Through massive ablation studies across architectures, objectives, datasets, and scaling parameters, T5 established that encoder-decoder networks outperform decoder-only ones for text generation when large inputs are encoded bidirectionally.

title: GPT-2 & Zero-Shot Task Transfer
summary: A 1.5-billion-parameter autoregressive Transformer language model that demonstrated tasks can be performed zero-shot — without any fine-tuning — purely by prompting the model to complete sequences.

papers.citation: 2019 · Alec Radford, Jeffrey Wu, Rewon Child, David Luan et al. · Language Models are Unsupervised Multitask Learners

sections.body:
A 1.5-billion-parameter autoregressive Transformer language model that demonstrated tasks can be performed zero-shot — without any fine-tuning — purely by prompting the model to complete sequences.

GPT-2 extended GPT-1's decoder-only Transformer from 117M to 1.5B parameters trained on WebText, a 40 GB internet text corpus. The conceptual breakthrough was reframing task-specific inference as an implicit sub-task of standard language modeling.

In standard supervised learning, a model predicts labels from input: \(P(\text{output} \mid \text{input})\). GPT-2 models the joint distribution autoregressively and conditions on a task description via prompt structure:

\[P(\text{output} \mid \text{input}, \text{task description})\]

Since the training corpus is large and extremely diverse, the standard language modeling objective already implicitly covers many translation and parsing tasks just to predict internet text sequences. The objective is simply Next-Token Prediction over sequences:

\[L = -\sum_{i} \log P(u_i \mid u_{i-k}, \ldots, u_{i-1}; \Theta)\]

When the model receives exactly the pattern "Translate English to French: [text] =>", the context biases the next-token attention matrices towards emitting French words because such structures occurred frequently on WebText.

On benchmark tasks like reading comprehension, translation, and text completion, GPT-2 established state-of-the-art results purely in the zero-shot setting against fully supervised models. Its capacity directly catalyzed the exploration of few-shot "in-context learning" fully popularized by GPT-3.

title: GPT-1 (Generative Pre-Training)
summary: The first model to demonstrate the generative "pre-train then fine-tune" paradigm for transformers: a decoder trained purely on language modeling unlabeled text, which is then fine-tuned on downstream data with minimal added layers.

papers.citation: 2018 · Alec Radford, Karthik Narasimhan, Tim Salimans, Ilya Sutskever · Improving Language Understanding by Generative Pre-Training

sections.body: The first model to demonstrate the generative "pre-train then fine-tune" paradigm for transformers: a decoder trained purely on language modeling unlabeled text, which is then fine-tuned on downstream data with minimal added layers.

Stage 1 — Unsupervised pre-training. Given an unlabeled corpus \(U = \{u_1, \ldots, u_n\}\), GPT-1 maximizes the standard causal language modeling objective:

\[L_1(U) = \sum_{i} \log P(u_i \mid u_{i-k}, \ldots, u_{i-1};\, \Theta)\]

The architecture is a 12-layer Transformer decoder with causal masked self-attention (768-dimensional hidden states, 12 attention heads), trained on the BooksCorpus (~7k unpublished books).

Stage 2 — Supervised fine-tuning. Given a labeled dataset with sequence inputs and targets \(C = \{(x_1 \dots x_m, y)\}\), it predicts \(y\) via a linear classification head applied on top of the last token's activation \(h_m^L\):

\[P(y \mid x^1, \ldots, x^m) = \text{softmax}(h_m^L W_y) \]

Crucially, an auxiliary language modeling task is preserved as a regularizing term alongside the classification objective, producing the combined training loss:

\[L_3(C) = L_2(C) + \lambda \cdot L_1(C)\]

This formulation allowed a single architecture to handle fundamentally disparate tasks (similarity vs classification vs comprehension) purely by converting them to bounded text sequences and fine-tuning. GPT-1 successfully dethroned previous recurrent/LSTM specific architectures across 9 benchmarks.

title: ELMo (Embeddings from Language Models)
summary: An influential technique that produces deep contextualized word embeddings by taking a learned linear combination of the internal hidden states of a pre-trained bidirectional LSTM language model.

papers.citation: 2018 · Matthew E. Peters, Mark Neumann, Mohit Iyyer, Matt Gardner et al. · Deep Contextualized Word Representations

sections.body:
An influential technique that produces deep contextualized word embeddings by taking a learned linear combination of the internal hidden states of a pre-trained bidirectional LSTM language model.

Prior to ELMo, word embeddings like Word2Vec and GloVe assigned a single fixed vector to each word type. The word "bank" received the exact same vector in "river bank" or "bank account", causing intractable polysemy. ELMo derived representations actively processing the specific surrounding sequence context using a bidirectional Language Model (biLM).

The biLM objective. The model predicts over the sequence to maximize both forward and backward causal objective:

\[J = \sum_{k=1}^{N} \Big( \log P(t_k \mid t_1, \ldots, t_{k-1}) + \log P(t_k \mid t_{k+1}, \ldots, t_N) \Big)\]

Task-specific combinations. For downstream tasks, ELMo doesn't just use the final layer. It learns a task-specific linear combination of all \(L\) layers:

\[\text{ELMo}_k^{\text{task}} = \gamma^{\text{task}} \sum_{j=0}^{L} s_j^{\text{task}} \, \mathbf{h}_{k,j}\]

where \(\mathbf{h}_{k,j}\) is the bidirectional hidden state at layer \(j\) for token \(k\), \(s_j^{\text{task}}\) are softmax mixture weights learned directly during tuning for the task, and \(\gamma^{\text{task}}\) is a scalar.

The paper famously highlighted that lower layers naturally captured syntactic properties (Part-of-Speech tags, structures), while upper layers captured deep semantic sense (Word Sense Disambiguation). It decisively proved that context-dependent deep pre-training representations transferred seamlessly and profoundly improved downstream tasks (+20% relative improvements in some tasks).

title: Sparsely-Gated Mixture of Experts (MoE)
summary: A sparsely-gated Mixture of Experts (MoE) conditional computation architecture in which a learned routing network directs each input token dynamically to only a small subset of parallel expert sub-networks, enabling exponential parameter…

papers.citation: 2017 · Noam Shazeer, Azalia Mirhoseini, Krzysztof Maziarz, Andy Davis, Quoc Le et al. · Outrageously Large Neural Networks: The Sparsely-Gated Mixture-of-Experts Layer

sections.body:
A sparsely-gated Mixture of Experts (MoE) conditional computation architecture in which a learned routing network directs each input token dynamically to only a small subset of parallel expert sub-networks, enabling exponential parameter scaling without proportional per-example computation.

Standard dense neural networks activate every parameter for every input. Mixture of Experts (MoE) breaks this constraint: the model contains \(n\) expert networks \(E_1, \ldots, E_n\) (typically block feed-forward layers), and a gating network \(G\) selects which experts process each individual input token. For an input \(x\), the output is:

\[y = \sum_{i=1}^{n} G(x)_i \cdot E_i(x)\]

where \(G(x) \in \mathbb{R}^n\) is sparse — most entries are forced to zero structurally. Sparsity is achieved by keeping only the top-\(k\) gate values (typically \(k = 1\) or \(2\)) and zeroing the rest unconditionally via a noisy top-k gating mechanism:

\[G(x) = \text{Softmax}\big(\text{TopK}(H(x), k)\big), \quad H(x)_i = (x \cdot W_g)_i + \epsilon_i \cdot \text{Softplus}((x \cdot W_{\text{noise}})_i)\]

where \(\epsilon_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0,1)\) is tunable exploration noise added during training to encourage diverse routing distributions across experts.

Load balancing. Without auxiliary intervention, the gating network collapses into constantly routing tokens purely to its favored few initial experts. To prevent it, an auxiliary importance loss / load-balancing loss penalizes the coefficient of variation of token distributions across the cluster, enforcing an active roughly equal fractional routing behavior.

The original Sparsely-Gated MoE inserted MoE between LSTMs achieving 137B scalable models before the concept ultimately merged perfectly with Transformers resulting in systems like Switch Transformer, NLLB, and Mixtral. This was vital in enabling huge models with constant inference speed due to sparsity.

title: Key-Value Memory Networks
summary: An influential evolution in Memory Networks that rigorously decouples the memory addressing mechanism (keys) from the memory payload returned (values), directly inspiring modern QKV attention mechanism formulations.

papers.citation: 2016 · Alexander H. Miller, Adam Fisch, Jesse Dodge, Antoine Bordes, Jason Weston · Key-Value Memory Networks for Directly Reading Documents

sections.body:
An influential evolution in Memory Networks that rigorously decouples the memory addressing mechanism (keys) from the memory payload returned (values), directly inspiring modern QKV attention mechanism formulations.

In the original End-to-End Memory Networks, each structural memory unit essentially held precisely one embedding representation used for interchangeably matching queries and producing read outputs. Key-Value Memory Networks bifurcate this systematically by storing completely disparate mappings per record as pure \((k_i, v_i)\) tuples. This explicit split allows optimal matching formats (ex: "Movie + Director" keys targeting user queries) to decouple from the actual retrieved payload facts (ex: "Wachowskis" string payloads).

Addressing phase (Key matching). Given a user query \(q\), the model explicitly derives fractional attention probabilties spanning solely over the memory keys leveraging standard normalized similarity comparisons:

\[p_i = \text{Softmax}(A \phi_X(x) \cdot A \phi_K(k_i))\]

where \(A\) behaves as heavily parameterized embedding representations and \(\phi_X, \phi_K\) establish mapping bounds.

Reading phase (Value aggregation). The final synthesized representation derives fully from a strictly linearly weighted ensemble mapping exactly from the independent value payloads using the attention bounds generated purely from the key evaluation:

\[o = \sum_i p_i \cdot A \phi_V(v_i)\]

This fundamentally decoupled Query-against-Key matching emitting precise independent Values became instantly isomorphic to the overarching Transformer Attention equation, bridging isolated QA memory paradigms dynamically into universal language modeling components.

Additionally, the architecture explicitly maintained deep multi-hop inference sequences iterating the exact Q-K-V evaluation stack to perform chaining across scattered entity relations without symbolic pipelines.

title: Luong Attention (Global and Local)
summary: A comprehensive pair of scalable Neural Machine Translation alignment attention mechanics—global attention traversing across all states and local attention selectively querying window domains—popularizing the extraordinarily scalable mul…

papers.citation: 2015 · Minh-Thang Luong, Hieu Pham, Christopher D. Manning · Effective Approaches to Attention-based Neural Machine Translation

sections.body:
A comprehensive pair of scalable Neural Machine Translation alignment attention mechanics—global attention traversing across all states and local attention selectively querying window domains—popularizing the extraordinarily scalable multiplicative dot-product score equations.

Luong explicitly documented and rigorously examined diverse core variants replacing the earlier Bahdanau additive formulas defining alignments crossing between target decoder bounds \( \mathbf{h}_t \) and encoder pools \( \bar{\mathbf{h}}_s \). The score functions became fundamentally standardized over this paper:

Name Score function
Dot \(\text{score}(\mathbf{h}_t, \bar{\mathbf{h}}_s) = \mathbf{h}_t^\top \bar{\mathbf{h}}_s\)
General (bilinear) \(\text{score}(\mathbf{h}_t, \bar{\mathbf{h}}_s) = \mathbf{h}_t^\top W_a \bar{\mathbf{h}}_s\)
Concat (additive) \(\text{score}(\mathbf{h}_t, \bar{\mathbf{h}}_s) = \mathbf{v}_a^\top \tanh(W_a [\mathbf{h}_t; \bar{\mathbf{h}}_s])\)

The matrix projection algorithms output softmax scalar evaluations traversing vectors to formulate standard summation vectors: \(\mathbf{c}_t = \sum_s \alpha_{ts} \bar{\mathbf{h}}_s\). Crucially, the simple Dot metric combined thoroughly into multiplicative computations enabling dense scalable highly optimized GPU matrix matrix routines (GEMM). This exact formulation dominates Transformer self-attention.

Global vs. local attention. A standard Global paradigm evaluates full bounds creating exhaustive \( O(N \times T) \) overheads heavily dragging long sequential processing arrays. Their local approximation predicted isolated focus segments—a temporal span \([p_t - D, p_t + D]\) augmented heavily using a spatial Gaussian bias. This approximated expensive hard-decisions via purely differentiable soft operations avoiding brittle reinforcement algorithms completely.

title: GloVe Word Embeddings
summary: An influential foundational word embedding paradigm optimizing directly over factorizing a full global document word-word co-occurrence matrix, achieving spectacular mathematical linearity across syntactic semantic analogies uniformly.

papers.citation: 2014 · Jeffrey Pennington, Richard Socher, Christopher D. Manning · GloVe: Global Vectors for Word Representation

sections.body:
An influential foundational word embedding paradigm optimizing directly over factorizing a full global document word-word co-occurrence matrix, achieving spectacular mathematical linearity across syntactic semantic analogies uniformly.

GloVe fundamentally shifts mapping operations natively starting across an inherently aggregated co-occurrence array \(X\), rather than raw stochastic window parsing typical inside Word2Vec. Word pair properties \(X_{ij}\) register absolute frequencies aggregating word \(j\) operating directly against context \(i\). A remarkable finding isolates intrinsic semantic meanings traversing over purely statistical ratio probabilities derived across contexts. Assessing elements like "ice" mapping via "solid" vs "gas", \(\frac{P(k \mid \text{solid})}{P(k \mid \text{gas})}\) drives massive discrepancies resolving specific traits.

Objective function. GloVe forces dot products explicitly approximating the global logarithms capturing co-occurrence spans minimizing:

\[J = \sum_{i,j=1}^{V} f(X_{ij}) \left( \mathbf{w}_i^\top \tilde{\mathbf{w}}_j + b_i + \tilde{b}_j - \log X_{ij} \right)^2\]

where \(\mathbf{w}_i\) bounds standard word entities against contextual representations \(\tilde{\mathbf{w}}_j\). \(f(X_{ij})\) supplies robust non-linear scaling constraints isolating frequent tokens like "the" from destroying precision:

\[f(x) = \begin{cases} (x / x_{\max})^\alpha & \text{if } x < x_{\max} \\ 1 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}\]

Using metrics precisely defined through \(\alpha = 0.75\), models learned perfectly generalized mathematical linear relationships: \(\mathbf{w}_{\text{king}} - \mathbf{w}_{\text{man}} + \mathbf{w}_{\text{woman}} \approx \mathbf{w}_{\text{queen}}\). 840B token pre-trained GloVe embeddings powered almost all sequence modeling applications fundamentally preceding pure contextual Transformers.

title: Neural Turing Machine (NTM)
summary: A novel deep differentiating computing architecture completely decoupling a central recurrent "Controller" module processing data dynamically actively modulating an adjacent structured global Memory bounds via differentiable read/write s…

papers.citation: 2014 · Alex Graves, Greg Wayne, Ivo Danihelka · Neural Turing Machines

sections.body:
A novel deep differentiating computing architecture completely decoupling a central recurrent "Controller" module processing data dynamically actively modulating an adjacent structured global Memory bounds via differentiable read/write soft access heads bridging algorithms entirely via end-to-end backpropagation gradients.

An NTM isolates discrete functionality components into a Controller network mapping operational commands evaluating explicitly defined constraints across a vast persistent memory matrix \(M \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times W}\) representing memory blocks holding generic wide data frames interacting fundamentally softly via complex Read and Write head distributions allowing exhaustive pure differentiability preserving loss flows.

Addressing mechanism. Heads generate fractional probabilistic attention vector sequences \(\mathbf{w}_t \in \mathbb{R}^N\) governing target interactions isolating slots fundamentally processing:

  1. Content-based addressing: Measuring explicit mapping distances enforcing standard cosine similarity metrics comparing dynamic search frames bounds \(\mathbf{k}_t\) traversing matrices actively enforcing scaling metrics: \[w_t^c(i) = \text{Softmax}\left(\beta_t \cdot \frac{\mathbf{k}_t \cdot M_t(i)}{\|\mathbf{k}_t\| \|M_t(i)\|}\right)\]
  2. Location-based addressing: Processing convolutions bridging temporal domains shifting locations \(-1, 0, +1\) natively permitting strictly continuous list iterations sequentially scanning memory sequences accurately bypassing arbitrary content bounds natively parsing lists fully structurally simulating iterators.
Reading phase: Vectors extract explicit linear probability aggregations evaluating exactly: \(\mathbf{r}_t = \sum_i w_t(i) \cdot M_t(i)\).

Writing phase: Emitting explicitly managed erase bindings \(\mathbf{e}_t\) modifying existing metrics conditionally adding new domains strictly \(\mathbf{a}_t\): \[\tilde{M}_t(i) = M_{t-1}(i) [\mathbf{1} - w_t(i) \mathbf{e}_t] + w_t(i) \mathbf{a}_t\]

Networks achieved unprecedented capability learning direct basic algorithm simulations encompassing binary arrays (copying algorithms), complex priority queues tracking values directly sorting dynamically. These differentiable memory algorithms heavily precipitated DNC structures fueling Transformer soft attention variants explicitly defining Key/Value paradigms fundamentally structurally mapping.
